summary_sections.title: Understanding Map Projections
summary_sections.content_html: Maps are two-dimensional representations of Earth's three-dimensional surface. All map projections introduce distortion because transferring a sphere onto a flat surface is mathematically impossible. No single map can simultaneously preserve shape (conformal), area (equal-area), distance, and direction. Mapmakers must choose what to prioritize, and these choices have consequences for how people perceive the world.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts and Vocabulary
summary_sections.content_html: Conformal projections preserve shape but distort area—the famous Mercator projection makes high latitudes appear larger than they are (Greenland appears larger than Africa despite Africa being 14 times bigger). Equal-area projections show correct relative sizes but distort shape. Equidistant projections maintain distance from one or two points. Different projections serve different purposes: Mercator for navigation, Robinson for general reference. The choice of projection reflects intent and introduces bias.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications and Examples
summary_sections.content_html: Real-world applications and consequences: Mercator's distortion has influenced global perceptions for centuries—the visual dominance of Europe and North America on widely-used maps reinforced colonial power relations; equal-area projections reveal Africa's true size and challenge Eurocentric worldviews; digital mapping uses different projections at different zoom levels; navigation systems use conformal projections; international organizations use various projections depending on purpose. Projection choice is inherently political.

summary_sections.title: Deeper Understanding
summary_sections.content_html: Always examine which projection a map uses—it shapes interpretation. Historical map projections like Mercator aren't 'wrong,' but they reflect particular choices and interests. Understanding projections requires appreciating that all maps involve trade-offs. The same geographic space can be represented very differently based on projection choice. Projection debate reveals how maps aren't neutral representations but convey value systems and power relations.
